title: Logging and Tracing (LangSmith/Phoenix)
concept: Tracing platforms record prompt-response paths and RAG retrieval chunks for every production query, enabling fast debugging of user complaints.

exercises.prompt: Why do developers use logging and tracing tools in AI applications?

options.text: See exactly what happened inside the AI steps when a user gets an error.
Make the AI generate answers instantly without thinking.
Translate your application code into different languages.

exercises.explain: When AI systems connect to databases (RAG), bugs can happen at any step. Tracing platforms help us see inside the process.

cards.title: Inside the AI Black Box
cards.body: When your AI is live, users will ask weird questions and get unexpected answers. Without a map of what happened inside your code, finding the bug is like finding a needle in a haystack. Tracing tools give you a replay button for every single AI interaction!

cards.title: What is AI Tracing?
cards.body: Tracing platforms like LangSmith and Arize Phoenix act as recorders for your AI app. They capture the complete journey of a user's request: the raw prompt, the specific chunks retrieved from your vector database, and the final response generated by the LLM. This makes debugging quick and visual.

cards.title: The Delivery Kitchen Log
cards.body: Imagine running a giant delivery kitchen. If a customer complains that their pizza arrived cold and missing toppings, you can't just guess what went wrong. You check the digital ticket log, which shows exactly when the order was placed, who prepped it, which oven it used, and how long the driver took. LangSmith and Phoenix act like this digital ticket system for your AI, tracking every step of a prompt's journey.

cards.title: Tracing in Action
cards.body:
A user asks, "How do I return a product?" and gets a bad answer. Using Phoenix, you open the trace log. You see:
1. Input: "How do I return a product?"
2. Retrieved Chunk: "Our office is closed on Sundays" (Wrong context!).
3. LLM Output: "We are closed Sundays."
Thanks to the trace, you instantly see the bug is in the search retriever, not the LLM.

cards.title: Myth: Standard Logs are Enough
cards.body: Many believe that standard system logs (like print statements) are enough for AI apps. However, standard logs only show text outputs. AI tracing tools show the entire tree of nested events, including API latency, token costs, and vector database search scores in one clean interface.

cards.title: Debugging: Blind vs. Guided
cards.body: Compare debugging an AI application with and without a dedicated tracing platform.

left.text: You only see the final wrong answer. You don't know if the database search failed or if the LLM ignored the instructions.

right.text: You see a timeline showing the exact retrieved chunks, prompt template inputs, latency, and costs for each query step.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of using LangSmith or Phoenix in an AI application?

options.text: To record and visualize the path of prompt-response queries for debugging.
To train new LLMs from scratch.
To design beautiful user interfaces for chatbot apps.
To block users from sending prompts to the AI.

exercises.explain: Tracing tools are designed to record the step-by-step path of AI prompts, database retrievals, and responses so developers can debug issues in production.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Tracing platforms can record prompts and responses from real users in production, not just during testing.
exercises.explain: Tracing platforms capture data in both development and live production environments so you can monitor real-world behavior and user complaints.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of AI debugging.
exercises.sentence: To find out why an AI gave a bad answer, developers check the visual ______ recorded by LangSmith or Phoenix.
exercises.explain: Tracing platforms record 'traces' which document the exact inputs, outputs, and intermediary steps (like RAG database lookups) for every live run.

exercises.prompt: A user complains that your AI support bot is answering with outdated pricing. How do you find out if the bot retrieved the wrong document?

options.text: Ask the customer to rewrite their question.
Open LangSmith or Phoenix to inspect the retrieved document chunks for that specific trace.

exercises.explain: Checking the trace history lets you see the actual input prompt, the search query sent to the vector database, and the retrieved context chunks, exposing exactly where the system retrieved the wrong data.

exercises.prompt: Your production RAG bot is suddenly giving incorrect answers to users. How does a tracing tool like LangSmith or Phoenix help you debug this issue?

options.text: It automatically rewrites your code to fix syntax errors.
It allows you to inspect the exact inputs, outputs, and retrieved chunks for every step of a query.
It makes the LLM run twice as fast by skipping steps.
It encrypts the prompts so even the developers cannot read them.

exercises.explain: When an LLM application behaves unexpectedly in production, tracing tools allow you to inspect the inputs and outputs of every internal step (like retriever queries and prompt templates) to pinpoint the exact failure point.
